title: Why Buildings Don't Fall Down
description: A beginner-friendly structural engineering Quiz about forces, load paths, and design trade-offs. It focuses on mental models: how bridges, towers, arches, floors, columns, and frames stay stable without leaning on formulas.
author: Quizbun

questions.title: What does `load path` mean in structural engineering?

options.text: The route forces take through structural elements until they reach the ground
The path people use to leave a building during an emergency
The order in which construction materials are delivered to the site
A decorative line that shows where a building looks visually heavy

questions.explanation: A load path is the chain of structural action from where a load enters the structure to where it is resisted, usually by the foundation and ground. For example, a floor load may move through floor boards, beams, columns, footings, and soil. If one link in that chain is missing or much weaker than assumed, the structure can fail even if the materials themselves are strong.

questions.title: Which are examples of loads a building may need to handle?

options.text: The building's own weight
People, furniture, or stored items
Wind, snow, rain, or earthquake effects
The color chosen for the facade

questions.explanation: Structural loads include dead loads, such as the permanent weight of the structure, live loads, such as people and movable contents, and environmental loads, such as wind, snow, rain, and earthquakes. Facade color may matter for heat, glare, or aesthetics, but it is not itself a structural load in the same sense.

questions.title: Which statement best distinguishes compression from tension?

options.text: Compression pushes material together; tension pulls material apart
Compression only happens in steel; tension only happens in concrete
Compression is a sideways force; tension is always a twisting force
Compression and tension are different names for the same load

questions.explanation: Compression is the inward pushing action that tends to shorten or squeeze a member. Tension is the pulling action that tends to stretch a member. Many structures deliberately pair materials and shapes with the force they handle well: masonry arches like compression, while cables are useful because they carry tension.

questions.title: In a suspension bridge, what force do the main hanging cables mainly carry?

options.text: Tension
Compression
Only pure bending
No force; the towers carry everything

questions.explanation: A cable works by being pulled. In a suspension bridge, the deck loads are carried by suspenders into the main cables, and the main cable tension is then transferred toward the anchorages and towers. The towers mostly take compression from the cable forces.

questions.title: Why is a triangle harder to deform than a rectangle made from the same pinned bars?

options.text: Once its side lengths are fixed, its shape is fixed
Triangles remove gravity from the structure
Triangles only work because they are usually drawn point-up
Triangles make every member weightless

questions.explanation: A pinned rectangle can rack into a parallelogram without changing the lengths of its sides. A triangle cannot do that: if all three side lengths are fixed, the angles are fixed too. That is why trusses commonly use triangular units to turn bending-prone frames into members that mainly carry tension or compression.

questions.title: A rectangular wall frame is easy to push into a parallelogram. What simple change usually makes it much stiffer sideways?

options.text: Add a diagonal brace so the rectangle becomes two triangles
Paint the frame a darker color
Make all joints freely rotate even more
Remove one side of the frame

questions.explanation: A diagonal brace blocks racking by creating triangular geometry. Instead of relying only on joint stiffness, the frame can send forces through the brace as tension or compression. This is the same basic idea behind many trusses and braced frames.

questions.title: In a traditional masonry arch, where does much of the load go?

options.text: Along the curved arch mostly as compression, then down and outward into the supports
Straight up into the air above the opening
Only into the keystone, where it disappears
Into tension in the stone blocks, which stone handles best

questions.explanation: A good arch redirects vertical load into compression along the curve. At the supports, that compression has a downward part and an outward horizontal thrust. This is why arches can span openings in materials like stone or brick, which are much better in compression than in tension.

questions.title: Why do arch bridges need strong abutments or an equivalent tie system?

options.text: The arch pushes outward as well as downward at its ends
The abutments make the arch lighter than air
The abutments stop all compression from entering the arch
Arches do not carry load unless the abutments are decorative

questions.explanation: An arch is not just a downward-load machine. It also creates horizontal thrust at its ends, so the supports must resist that push or the arch can spread. A tied arch solves a similar problem by using a tension member to hold the arch ends together.

questions.title: What is the foundation's role in the load path?

options.text: Transfer loads from the structure into soil or rock safely enough to avoid instability or harmful settlement
Make the upper floors independent of gravity
Only hold the building's plumbing in place
Carry only wind loads, never vertical loads

questions.explanation: The foundation is the last major link in the load path before the ground. It spreads or carries loads into soil or rock while controlling settlement, sliding, and overturning risks. Poor soil or very high loads may require deeper foundations such as piles.

questions.title: A simply supported floor beam bends downward under a load in the middle. What usually happens inside the beam?

options.text: The top region is mostly in compression and the bottom region is mostly in tension
The entire beam is weightless because it bends
Only the paint layer carries tension and compression
Bending creates no internal forces

questions.explanation: When a simple floor beam sags, the upper fibers shorten and the lower fibers stretch. That means the top region is mainly in compression and the bottom region is mainly in tension. Real beams may also carry shear, but the compression-tension pair is the core bending mental model.

questions.title: Why is an I-beam efficient for carrying many floor loads?

options.text: It places much of the material far from the neutral axis, where it helps resist bending
It hides the load inside the hollow letter shape
It works because the letter I has no structural forces
It turns every floor load into sound instead of stress

questions.explanation: In bending, material far from the neutral axis does more work against tension and compression. An I-beam concentrates material in the flanges, away from the neutral axis, while the web helps carry shear and hold the flanges apart. It is efficient for one main bending direction, not magic for every possible load.

questions.title: Which statements correctly distinguish strength, stiffness, and stability?

options.text: Strength is about resisting failure such as yielding, crushing, or breaking
Stiffness is about resisting deformation under load
Stability includes avoiding shape-change failures such as buckling
If something is strong, it is automatically stiff and stable in every geometry

questions.explanation: These are related but not identical. A member can be strong enough not to crush, but still too flexible for comfort or too slender to stay stable. Structural design checks several limits: material strength, deflection or vibration, and stability modes such as buckling.

questions.title: A long, thin column is compressed. What failure mode can occur before the material is crushed?

options.text: Buckling: it suddenly bows sideways and loses load-carrying capacity
It becomes a cable and carries the load in tension
It stops experiencing gravity
It must always split evenly into two shorter columns

questions.explanation: Buckling is a stability failure, not simply a material-strength failure. A slender column can bow sideways at a critical load even when the stress is below the material's crushing or yielding strength. That is why column length, end restraint, straightness, and bracing matter so much.

questions.title: Why can a tall building be designed to sway a little instead of being perfectly rigid?

options.text: Controlled movement can let the structure handle wind or earthquakes while keeping stresses and occupant motion within limits
Sway proves the building has no loads
A swaying building never needs stiffness or damping
Perfect rigidity is always cheaper and safer for tall buildings

questions.explanation: Tall buildings are dynamic systems. Designers manage strength, stiffness, damping, and human comfort rather than trying to make an impossibly motionless object. Too much movement can be unsafe or uncomfortable, but controlled flexibility plus damping can be better than simply adding material everywhere.

questions.title: What is the main job of a tuned mass damper in a tall building or bridge?

options.text: Reduce vibration amplitude by moving in a tuned way that adds damping to the system
Remove all gravity loads from the structure
Replace foundations with a hanging weight
Make the building lighter by deleting columns

questions.explanation: A tuned mass damper is an added mass on springs, pendulums, or dampers, tuned to reduce unwanted vibration. It does not erase the load path or remove the need for strong structure. It helps control motion from wind, earthquakes, machinery, or pedestrian effects.

questions.title: Why can resonance be dangerous for bridges or buildings?

options.text: Repeated forces near a structure's natural frequency can build up large vibrations
Resonance means a structure has no natural frequency
Resonance only happens in musical instruments, never in structures
Resonance prevents motion instead of amplifying it

questions.explanation: Resonance is about timing. If repeated forces arrive near a structure's natural frequency, energy can build up and produce large motion. Engineers avoid this by changing stiffness or mass, adding damping, or avoiding likely forcing frequencies. The London Millennium Bridge is a useful footbridge example; Tacoma Narrows is often taught with resonance, but its collapse is more accurately associated with aeroelastic flutter, so it should be used carefully.
questions.references: [Wikipedia: Mechanical resonance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mechanical_resonance) · [Wikipedia: Millennium Bridge, London](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Millennium_Bridge,_London) · [Wikipedia: Tacoma Narrows Bridge (1940)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tacoma_Narrows_Bridge_%281940%29)

questions.title: Which choices can help a structure resist sideways forces like wind or earthquake effects?

options.text: Braced frames or shear walls that create clear lateral load paths
Connections that can transfer the intended forces between members
A foundation system able to resist overturning, sliding, and settlement
Ignoring horizontal loads because gravity is vertical

questions.explanation: Sideways loads still need a load path. Wind and earthquakes can move forces through floors, bracing, shear walls, frames, connections, and foundations. If the lateral system is discontinuous or the foundation cannot resist the effects, the building may be vulnerable even if its vertical columns are strong.

questions.title: What is the purpose of a factor of safety in engineering design?

options.text: Provide capacity beyond the expected maximum load to account for uncertainty, misuse, degradation, or emergency conditions
Let engineers skip structural analysis
Guarantee that any structure can never fail
Make the structure exactly as strong as the everyday load and no stronger

questions.explanation: A factor of safety expresses capacity beyond a specified load or a required design margin. It is not permission to guess, and it cannot guarantee absolute safety. It is one way engineers manage uncertainty in loads, materials, construction quality, future wear, and consequences of failure.

questions.title: What two-word term describes the route loads take through a structure?
questions.explanation: The term is `load path`. It is a compact way to ask, 'Where does the force go next?' Good structural thinking keeps following that route through members, connections, foundations, and ground.
